abstract:
This paper introduces a quantum algorithm for the support vector machine (SVM), a fundamental tool in supervised machine learning for binary classification. By leveraging quantum properties, the proposed method achieves a complexity that is logarithmic in both the dimensionality of the feature space ($N$) and the number of training examples ($M$). This represents an exponential speedup over classical algorithms in scenarios where high-dimensional data or large datasets typically require polynomial time.

The technical foundation of this "big data" algorithm rests on a reformulation of the SVM as a least-squares problem, which allows the classification task to be solved via quantum matrix inversion. Central to this approach is a novel technique for the exponentiation of non-sparse matrices, which enables the efficient manipulation of the training data's kernel matrix. The authors demonstrate that this process effectively performs a quantum principal component analysis (PCA), identifying the most significant features of the dataset while maintaining an efficient runtime. Furthermore, the paper describes how the algorithm can be extended to non-linear classification using polynomial kernels by mapping data into high-dimensional tensor product spaces. Beyond computational efficiency, the quantum SVM offers inherent advantages for data privacy, as it processes the statistical structure of the data without requiring the explicit representation of individual training features.
